title: Server Actions

learningObjectives.text: Explain server-only mutations using a new example and justify the result.
Explain `use server` using a new example and justify the result.
Explain forms and mutations using a new example and justify the result.
Explain interaction with client components using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: This topic turns server actions from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **Server and Client Architecture** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Server-only mutations** → **`use server`** → **Forms and mutations** → **Interaction with client components**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Server-only mutations
sections.body: A Server Action is a server function used for a mutation, often through forms or client invocation.

sections.title: `use server`
sections.body: The directive marks server functions, not ordinary Server Components.

sections.title: Forms and mutations
sections.body: Forms can invoke Server Actions while preserving progressive enhancement. Validate authorization and input on the server.

sections.title: Interaction with client components
sections.body: Client Components can submit to or invoke a Server Action while the function itself executes on the server.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: The server action performs a mutation and revalidates affected data.

commonMistakes: Marking a large route tree as client code when only a small interaction needs it.
Confusing React Server Components with server-side rendering.
Assuming cached data is fresh without an explicit policy and invalidation path.
Placing security-sensitive validation only in the client or middleware layer.
summary: Server Actions connects Server-only mutations, `use server`, Forms and mutations.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Server Actions in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Apply Server-only mutations deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Server Actions.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Server Actions.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Server Actions.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which response best addresses this common mistake: “Marking a large route tree as client code when only a small interaction needs it.”?

choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.
choices.feedback: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.

choices.label: The statement is correct only when variable names are short.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Server Actions.

choices.label: The statement is unrelated to program behavior and cannot cause defects.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Server Actions.

choices.label: The statement is always correct, so no further reasoning about Server Actions is needed.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Server Actions.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Server Actions?

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Server-only mutations, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Server Actions.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Server Actions.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Server Actions.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Server Actions?

choices.label: Avoid Server Actions completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Server Actions.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Server Actions.

choices.label: Use Server Actions in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Server Actions.

choices.label: Use Server Actions when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
